sitting_overview: The Transport Secretary set out a reset of HS2, putting the cost of completion at £87.7 billion to £102.7 billion and the first London to Birmingham services between 2036 and 2039. An urgent question saw the Intelligence and Security Committee accuse the Government of redacting and withholding documents beyond the terms of the Mandelson Humble Address, and the fourth day of the King's Speech debate, on energy security, divided the House over the Bill to end new North Sea oil and gas licences.

highlights.summary: The Transport Secretary, Heidi Alexander, put the cost of completing HS2 at £87.7 billion to £102.7 billion and said the first trains between London and Birmingham would not run until between 2036 and 2039.
highlights.key_quote: I can today confirm that the previous Government spent most of HS2's budget without laying a single metre of its track.
highlights.analysis: Alexander published the Lovegrove report alongside HS2's revised cost and schedule. She attributed two thirds of the cost increase to underestimation and inefficiency and one third to inflation, and said the line's design speed would be cut to 320 kph to save up to £2.5 billion. She told the House that cancelling HS2 could cost almost as much as completing it.

debate_map.quote: Errors were made and should be rectified, and I am glad that the Government continue to support Mark Wild and his team as they work towards opening HS2.
Will she assure me that she will not put on the high-vis and the hard hat for the photo opportunities until all the detail, permits and cost budgets are in place first?
high-speed rail focuses not on going faster, but on going further

highlights.summary: Sir Jeremy Wright (CON), chair of the Intelligence and Security Committee, said the Government intended to redact and withhold documents beyond the terms of the Mandelson Humble Address, which he called a question of parliamentary sovereignty.
highlights.key_quote: It has become apparent to us that the Government also intend to redact documents for other reasons not specifically permitted in the Humble Address or, in some cases, to withhold documents altogether.
highlights.analysis: The Humble Address agreed in February required documents on Peter Mandelson's appointment to be referred to the Intelligence and Security Committee, with redactions limited to national security or international relations grounds. Wright, the Committee's chair, said the Government was also redacting on other grounds and withholding some documents entirely, and asked it to seek the House's consent for any change. Darren Jones said the Government had complied with the Address and would publish a second tranche of material after the Whitsun recess.

debate_map.quote: In response to the questions from the right hon. and learned Gentleman about compliance with the Humble Address, I refer him to the statement that I have made previously in relation to the principles set out in legislation and the motions of the House.
if the Humble Address motion does not allow for redaction on those grounds, the Government cannot just unilaterally decide to ignore the will of the House
We have just debated an area where the Government may potentially be in contempt of the House; indeed, the obfuscation we have heard looks like that.

highlights.debate: Debate on the Address: Energy Security
highlights.summary: The shadow Energy Secretary, Claire Coutinho, moved the Opposition's amendment against the planned ban on new North Sea oil and gas licences, arguing it would raise Britain's dependence on imported energy.
highlights.key_quote: It is an energy dependence Bill that would leave us weaker, poorer and more reliant on foreign regimes.
highlights.analysis: Coutinho moved the Opposition's amendment to the Humble Address, opposing the planned end to new North Sea oil and gas licences, the cancellation of a third large nuclear plant at Wylfa, and the carbon tax regime. She argued that cutting domestic production would not reduce consumption but increase imports. The Government's position is that the price of oil and gas is set on international markets, so new licences would not lower bills.

debate_map.quote: Will she tell us by how much our bills were reduced as a result of having our own oil and gas when the Ukraine crisis happened?
Her position now is that she does not believe in net zero, and does not believe that it is desirable or achievable.
our “get Britain drilling” Bill is vital not just for energy security but for our future economic security

highlights.summary: Emily Thornberry (LAB), chair of the Foreign Affairs Committee, said the security vetting document on Peter Mandelson had recommended against his appointment, and asked how that had become a decision to appoint him.
highlights.key_quote: why, when the United Kingdom Security Vetting document had two red boxes ticked, including “This man should not be appointed”, was that somehow or other translated into “He should be appointed”?
highlights.analysis: Thornberry said the security vetting summary on Mandelson had carried a recommendation against his appointment, which the Foreign Office overrode. She argued the Intelligence and Security Committee should be allowed to see the file, with redactions, to establish how the appointment was approved. Darren Jones said the recommendation and the Foreign Office's decision had been referred to the Committee, but that the raw vetting interview data would not be shared.

debate_map.quote: What has not been referred is the raw data collected as part of interviews undertaken with Peter Mandelson, which in any circumstances we would not share in relation to any appointment.
In more than 30 years of its existence, the Intelligence and Security Committee has never once suffered from a leak.

highlights.debate: Debate on the Address: Energy Security
highlights.summary: The Energy Secretary, Ed Miliband, defended the ban on new oil and gas licences by quoting Coutinho's own 2023 words back to her in support of home-grown clean power.
highlights.key_quote: I agree with her. The problem is that she no longer agrees with herself.
highlights.analysis: Miliband read back to Coutinho words she had used as Energy Secretary in 2023, when she argued that home-grown clean power protected consumers from fossil fuel market volatility. He said new licences would, according to the National Energy System Operator, make no material difference to security of supply and would not reduce bills. He defended retaining the windfall tax on oil and gas profits during the conflict in the Middle East.

debate_map.quote: Why has he not published one?

If it can be demonstrated that UK consumption of oil and gas is not falling at a rate that is equal to, or faster than, the rate of production in the United Kingdom, will he release his screeching U-turn on new oil and gas licences in the North sea?
does the Secretary of State understand that the tax does not apply to trading nor to overseas production?

highlights.summary: Gregory Stafford (CON) challenged the Justice Secretary over plans to restrict jury trials, citing Bar Council and Institute for Government estimates that the change would save little court time.
highlights.key_quote: The backlog was not caused by juries and it will not be solved by scrapping juries.
highlights.analysis: The Government's Courts and Tribunals Bill would restrict the right to a jury trial for some offences. Stafford cited the Bar Council and the Institute for Government, which estimated the change would save at most about 2% of court time, and argued the Crown court backlog was not caused by juries. Lammy said the backlog stood at more than 80,000 cases and that reform, alongside investment, was needed to reduce it.

debate_map.quote: We are not scrapping juries; juries remain a cornerstone of our system.
When is the Justice Secretary going to get his ego out the way and bin the ludicrous idea of curtailing jury trials?
At the Old Bailey in 2025, the backlog fell by a quarter, in Chelmsford it fell by 10%, and Maidstone saw a 5% reduction.

highlights.summary: Joe Robertson (CON) told the House that 441 prisoners had been released in error since July 2024, which he said was the worst figure on record, after the minister declined to give a number.
highlights.key_quote: It is 441, which over a two-year period is the worst on record.
highlights.analysis: Asked for the number of prisoners released in error since July 2024, the minister, Jake Richards, said data would be published in due course. Robertson put the figure at 441, which he said was the worst on record over a two-year period. Richards said no comparable data had been published under the previous Government, and that the Government had accepted the recommendations of Dame Lynne Owens' independent review.

debate_map.quote: in 14 years the Conservative Government never released data on prisoner releases in error, so we will take no lectures from the Opposition Benches on that issue

How many of those 441 prisoners, wrongly let out of prison on Labour's watch, remain at large, and why is it still taking days for the Prison Service to let the police know when it has wrongly released people?

highlights.debate: Debate on the Address: Energy Security
highlights.summary: Katie Lam (CON) set out the case that more domestic gas production would lower household heating bills, on the basis that gas is a partly localised market.
highlights.key_quote: If we produced more gas domestically, it would be cheaper to buy gas, meaning that heating bills would, in fact, come down.
highlights.analysis: Lam argued that gas, particularly liquefied natural gas shipped from abroad, is priced partly by the distance it travels, so that more domestic production would lower the cost of supply to Britain. The Government and several Members cited the National Energy System Operator and the International Energy Agency in arguing that new North Sea production would not materially affect prices or security of supply. The exchange turned on whether domestic gas output affects the price British households pay.

debate_map.quote: expansion of production of North sea oil and gas does not significantly improve the UK's energy security

highlights.summary: Adam Jogee (LAB) said constituents had been forced into bankruptcy while waiting years for HS2 compensation for the compulsory purchase of their land.
highlights.key_quote: my constituents Mr and Mrs Kettering, of Madeley, have been waiting years for the compensation due to them for the compulsory purchase of their land? They have been forced to declare bankruptcy because they have been waiting so long.
highlights.analysis: Jogee raised constituents whose land was subject to compulsory purchase for HS2 and who, he said, had been forced into bankruptcy while awaiting compensation. Other Members said the community and business funds set up to support affected areas remained largely unspent. The minister, Lilian Greenwood, said HS2's compliance with its undertakings had fallen short in under 1% of cases and that the Department was committed to resolving outstanding disputes.

debate_map.quote: The failure to purchase land has forced residents into bankruptcy and caused relationship breakdowns.
Millions of pounds are still unspent, despite our communities being blighted.
out of nearly 5,000 undertakings and assurances on HS2, compliance has fallen short in less than 1% of cases, but that is 1% too many

highlights.summary: Alicia Kearns (CON) raised a constituent whose abuser, two years into a nine-year sentence, had been moved to an open prison with the right to leave the site and stay out overnight.
highlights.key_quote: my constituent who received a call in January and was told that her abuser, the paedophile, who was only two years into a nine-year sentence, had been moved into an open prison, and now had the right to leave the site freely, and even stay out overnight.
highlights.analysis: Kearns said a constituent had been told that the man who abused her, two years into a nine-year sentence, had been moved to an open prison with the right to leave the site and stay out overnight. The minister, Jake Richards, said prisoners convicted of sexual offences move to open conditions only after an individual risk assessment, and can be returned to closed conditions if their risk increases. He agreed to look into the case.

debate_map.quote: I am very happy to look into that case and make sure that the appropriate officials look into it too.

highlights.summary: Dr Allison Gardner (LAB) pressed for a £100 million expansion of GPS tagging to cover all convicted domestic abusers, not only the highest-risk offenders.
highlights.key_quote: I welcome the announcement of a record £100 million expansion of GPS tagging for domestic abusers following a pilot by the Greater London Authority that saw reoffending fall by up to 63%.
highlights.analysis: Gardner welcomed a £100 million expansion of GPS tagging for domestic abusers, citing a London pilot in which reoffending fell by up to 63%, but said applying it only to the highest-risk offenders risked leaving others untracked. The minister, Catherine Atkinson, said a domestic abuse perpetrator tagging pilot would be rolled out nationally from September. The exchange came as Baroness Harman takes up a new role as the Prime Minister's adviser on women and girls.

debate_map.quote: the DAPOL—domestic abuse perpetrators on licence—pilot that will be rolled out nationally from September

highlights.summary: The shadow Justice Secretary, Nick Timothy, asked the Justice Secretary directly whether as many knife offenders would be jailed next year as last, and did not get a figure.
highlights.key_quote: As the Government scrapped almost all sentences of less than a year, will the Justice Secretary say very clearly whether he expects as many knife criminals to go to jail next year as did last year?
highlights.analysis: Timothy asked the Justice Secretary directly whether he expected as many knife offenders to be jailed next year as last, given the Government has ended most sentences of under a year. Lammy did not give a figure, instead pointing to a fall in knife crime over the past year and the youth justice White Paper published the previous day. Timothy argued the White Paper pointed to lower imprisonment rates.

debate_map.quote: I am pleased that after 22 months in office, we have seen falls in knife crime in the last year
the challenge of knife crime starts with its root causes, which include poverty and a lack of opportunity for young people

highlights.debate: Debate on the Address: Energy Security
highlights.summary: Sir Edward Leigh (CON), the Father of the House, called for the pensions triple lock to be changed to an average of its three measures, and told his own front bench not to oppose it.
highlights.key_quote: Yes, we want to keep a triple lock, but not the triple lock.
highlights.analysis: Leigh, the Father of the House, used the King's Speech debate to call for the pensions triple lock to be changed to a single average of its three measures, rather than the highest of the three each year. He said the current formula was financially unsustainable as the ratio of working taxpayers to pensioners falls. He urged the Government to act and said his own front bench should not oppose the change.

highlights.debate: Debate on the Address: Energy Security
highlights.summary: Nadia Whittome (LAB) openly criticised her own Government's strategy from the back benches, saying its approach to immigration had broken Labour's voter coalition.
highlights.key_quote: We have smashed apart our own voter coalition, and as we haemorrhage votes to the Greens, we are also delivering seats to Reform.
highlights.analysis: Whittome, a Labour backbencher, used the King's Speech debate to criticise her own Government's strategy, saying its approach to immigration had cost it support and that the local election results showed the need for a change of course. She called for rent controls and public ownership of utilities. Her speech was an open expression of dissent from the Government benches over the direction set out in the King's Speech.

debate_map.quote: We need to be ambitious, and we need to be looking at the big picture and fixing systems as well as situations.
She is right: this is a very underwhelming King's Speech.

highlights.debate: Debate on the Address: Energy Security
highlights.summary: Tim Farron (LD) rejected the framing of Britain as a broken country, arguing against what he called an aggressive and malign nostalgia.
highlights.key_quote: We are not broken, but we must be better.
highlights.analysis: Farron rejected the description of Britain as broken, which several Members across the debate had used, calling it a corrosive form of nostalgia. He argued the country faced real challenges on energy, food and defence, but was not in terminal decline. The Energy Minister, Michael Shanks, singled out the speech approvingly when winding up the debate.

debate_map.quote: He emphasised absolutely rightly that Britain is not broken but that we must be better.

sections.summary: The House met at half-past eleven, with prayers.
The House ordered a new writ for a by-election in the Makerfield constituency, following the appointment of the former Member to an office of profit under the Crown.
The Speaker announced that the ballot for private Members' Bills was open for signatures and would be drawn that Thursday.

Oral questions to the Justice Secretary and his ministers, covering family courts, reoffending, violence against women and girls, jury trial changes, open prisons, knife crime, the victims code, the criminalisation of children in care, support for men and boys, and prisoners released in error. The Lord Chancellor, David Lammy, defended proposed changes to jury trials as necessary to reduce a Crown court backlog of more than 80,000 cases, and extended the family mediation voucher scheme for a further year. Ministers confirmed a £100 million expansion of GPS tagging for domestic abusers and a national roll-out of domestic abuse perpetrator tagging from September, and faced questions on a youth justice White Paper published the previous day.

An urgent question to the Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster, Darren Jones, on the redaction and withholding of documents within the scope of the Humble Address agreed by the House on 4 February. The chair of the Intelligence and Security Committee, Sir Jeremy Wright, said the Government intended to redact documents on grounds not permitted by the Address and to withhold some entirely, which he argued required the consent of the House. Jones said the Government had complied with the Address, that all documents needing referral to the Committee had been referred, and that a second tranche of material would be published after the Whitsun recess. David Davis gave notice that he might table a contempt motion if the Address was not met in full.
The Speaker announced that Bob Blackman had been re-elected unopposed as Chair of the Backbench Business Committee.

A statement by the Transport Secretary, Heidi Alexander, publishing the Lovegrove report and revised figures for HS2. She put the cost of completion at between £87.7 billion and £102.7 billion in 2025 prices, said the first services between Old Oak Common and Birmingham were expected between May 2036 and October 2039, and announced that the line's design speed would be reduced to 320 kph. She said cancelling the project could cost almost as much as completing it. The shadow rail minister, Jerome Mayhew, said errors had been made and should be rectified, and pressed for detail on what the new figures included.
The fourth day of debate on the Humble Address in response to the King's Speech resumed, with the day's debate focused on energy security.

The fourth day of the King's Speech debate, on energy security. The shadow Energy Secretary, Claire Coutinho, moved an Opposition amendment opposing the planned ban on new North Sea oil and gas licences and the cancellation of a third large nuclear plant at Wylfa, and calling for the carbon tax regime to be abolished. The Energy Secretary, Ed Miliband, defended the energy independence Bill as the response to a second fossil fuel price shock, arguing that new licences would not cut bills or materially affect security of supply. Members divided over whether domestic oil and gas production lowers prices, the future of North Sea jobs, nuclear power, and a planned ban on fracking, and speeches ranged more widely across the pensions triple lock, water industry reform, electoral reform and the state of the two main parties. The amendment was put to a division.
Wendy Chamberlain presented a petition from 585 residents of North East Fife calling for a cash or banking hub in Cupar, following the closure of local bank branches.
Vikki Slade presented a petition from residents of Holton Heath Park in Mid Dorset and North Poole calling for action on antisocial driving, including acoustic cameras and increased police patrols.

An adjournment debate led by Joy Morrissey on the effect of HS2 construction on communities along the route. Members from several parties raised damage to roads, delayed compensation for compulsory land purchase, properties left empty by the cancelled northern sections, and unspent community funds. The Transport Minister, Lilian Greenwood, said a highways deterioration fund had been established, that compliance had fallen short in under 1% of HS2's undertakings, and that the Department was committed to resolving outstanding cases.
